title: Tropicalizing Principal Minors of Positive Definite Matrices
speaker: Josephine Yu
abstract: We study the tropicalization of principal minors of positive definite matrices over a real valued field. This tropicalization forms a subset of M-concave functions on the discrete n-dimensional cube. We show that it coincides with a linear slice of a larger tropical Grassmannian, and also with the portion of the affine tropical Flag variety inside the submodular cone.  By lifting tropical inequalities, we uncover new polynomial inequalities on the principal minors, and more generally, on coefficients of Lorentzian polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Positive definite principal-minor map
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes positive definite real or Hermitian matrices, defines their principal minors and the principal-minor map, and recalls classical constraints such as positivity, semialgebraicity, and submodularity of logarithmic principal minors.

proof_stages.label: Puiseux-series tropicalization of the image
proof_stages.summary: The principal-minor map is moved to real or complex Puiseux series with the max-tropical valuation, yielding a tropical image/log-limit set that is a polyhedral fan and still lies in the submodular cone.

proof_stages.label: Stable-polynomial route to M-natural concavity
proof_stages.summary: Principal minors are encoded as the coefficients of a stable determinant polynomial, so Brändén’s theorem on valuations of Lorentzian/stable polynomials implies that tropical principal minors form M-natural-concave functions and motivates the realizability problem.

proof_stages.label: Equivalent models for M-concave functions
proof_stages.summary: M-natural concavity is reviewed through the exchange axiom, the multisymmetric lift to the Dressian, homogenized two-layer Dressian conditions, the affine flag Dressian, and regular subdivisions of the cube, highlighting the representable versions of these characterizations.

proof_stages.label: Representability theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that, up to an overall scaling, tropical principal minors of positive definite matrices are exactly those functions whose multisymmetric lift lies in the tropical Grassmannian, equivalently the submodular points in the tropical affine flag variety; representability of all two-layer slices follows but the converse is expected to fail.

proof_stages.label: Consequences for small n and dimension
proof_stages.summary: Known results on tropical flag varieties imply equality with all M-concave functions for n up to five but not for n at least six, and the theorem gives the dimension as that of the complete flag variety with one extra scaling parameter, also over the real Puiseux field.

proof_stages.label: Proof via Cauchy–Binet, mixing, and flags
proof_stages.summary: The proof sketch uses A=B*B and Cauchy–Binet to express principal minors as positive sums with no valuation cancellation, applies a generic row-mixing lemma to construct the required multisymmetric Grassmannian point, reverses the construction by B*B, and relates the Grassmannian and affine-flag realizations, with the flag-to-Grassmannian direction requiring a hard submodularity lemma.

proof_stages.label: Tropical equations explained by lifted inequalities
proof_stages.summary: The final discussion explains why semialgebraic tropicalizations can satisfy tropical Plücker equations even without algebraic equations upstairs: each tropical equality is decomposed into tropical inequalities that lift to sharp algebraic inequalities for principal minors, more generally for the corresponding Lorentzian polynomials.

examples.name: Gaussian covariance and multi-information
examples.note: Positive definite matrices are recalled as covariance/correlation matrices of multivariate Gaussians, where logarithms of principal minors encode mutual or multi-information and make submodularity familiar.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker invokes Gaussian covariance matrices and multi-information to motivate principal minors and states their supermodularity, but does not compute or verify anything for a concrete Gaussian example.

examples.name: The three-dimensional cube and octahedral tropical Plücker relation
examples.note: For functions on subsets of a three-element set, the cube layers and middle octahedron make the M-concavity/tropical Plücker condition and its inequality lifts concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes to the n=3 cube, identifies the middle six vertices as an octahedron, writes the tropical Plücker maximum condition on its diagonals, and translates it into tropical/algebraic inequalities.

passes.reason: The cube/octahedron is a regular highly symmetric configuration, used to show algebraically independent principal minors can nevertheless satisfy a tropical Plücker relation rather than an algebraic equation.
The 3D cube/octahedron is a regular highly symmetric local Plücker configuration, used here to show that the tropical Plücker equation appears even though no algebraic equation exists upstairs.
The cube/octahedron is a maximally regular polyhedral configuration used to show that the tropical Plücker condition need not come from algebraic equations.

The cube/octahedron is a regular highly symmetric polytope, and it is used to show that algebraically independent minors can nevertheless satisfy a tropical Plücker relation, so an equations-upstairs approach fails.
The 3-cube/octahedron configuration is a regular highly symmetric polyhedral model illustrating the tropical Plücker relation, not refuting a claim.

The cube/octahedron is a regular highly symmetric configuration, and the n=3 case is used to show tropical Plücker relations can arise even though no algebraic equation among the relevant minors exists.

The regular cube/octahedron configuration is used to show that a tropical Plücker equation can appear even though the corresponding principal minors are algebraically independent, so equations alone cannot explain the semialgebraic tropicalization.
The 3-cube’s middle vertices forming a regular octahedron illustrate the tropical Plücker/M-concavity condition, and the cube/octahedron are highly regular symmetric polytopes.
The 3-cube and its middle-layer octahedron are regular highly symmetric polytopes used illustratively to display the tropical Plücker relation.
The regular cube/octahedron configuration is used to show that a tropical Plücker relation can appear despite algebraic independence upstairs, obstructing an equation-lifting explanation.

The 3-cube/middle octahedron is a regular highly symmetric polytope used to illustrate the octahedral tropical Plücker condition rather than as an adversarial counterexample.
